one_line_takeaway: A literature survey -- not an empirical study -- that organizes existing LLM-agent security research into a four-layer taxonomy (context, tool, state, ecosystem), finding via its own tabulation that 13 of 19 reviewed studies cluster on the context/tool layers while state and ecosystem risks (2 studies each) remain comparatively unstudied, and illustrates cross-layer propagation with a hypothetical, non-executed OpenClaw attack scenario built from public documentation.
key_finding: The paper's own synthesis (Section 5.1, based on its Table 6 count of 19 representative studies) reports that the context and tool layers together account for 13 of 19 studies, while the state layer and ecosystem layer receive only 2 studies each -- i.e., research attention is heavily skewed toward prompt-level and tool-level threats and comparatively neglects persistent-state and ecosystem-level risks. It also reports that only 2 of the 19 studies propose a full defense mechanism (TrustRAG and TrustAgent, per the paper's classification), with 2 more (AgentDojo and ASB) classified as offering only partial defenses. From this the authors argue that current agent-security defenses are largely layer-isolated (e.g., prompt filtering cannot stop injected content from later biasing tool selection or persisting in memory) and that the literature lacks long-horizon, multi-session evaluation of compounding/persistent attack effects and lacks enforceable, fine-grained ecosystem trust models for plugins/skills/automation.
core_contribution: A survey (not an empirical study) that proposes a four-layer security taxonomy for autonomous LLM agent frameworks -- context and instruction layer, tool and action layer, state and persistence layer, and ecosystem and automation layer -- and uses it to organize existing agent-security literature. For each layer the paper summarizes the layer's functional role, catalogs representative attacks and defenses drawn from prior published work, and discusses attack-defense gaps. It then synthesizes a cross-layer view: a literature distribution table (Table 6, 19 studies), an analysis of how attacks propagate context -> tool -> state -> ecosystem, and a hypothetical (author-constructed, not executed) cross-layer attack walkthrough using OpenClaw's publicly documented mechanisms (runtime prompt assembly, typed tools, agent workspace, heartbeat/cron automation) as a case study. The paper closes with three open problems the authors identify: research imbalance across layers, lack of long-horizon evaluation, and weak ecosystem trust models. The stated contributions (per the paper's own summary) are: (1) a unified layered taxonomy for analyzing agent security, (2) a synthesis of existing threat/defense studies across architectural levels, and (3) identification of cross-layer threat propagation and research gaps.
threat_model: The paper does not define a single formal threat model of its own since it is a literature survey; instead it characterizes, layer by layer, threat models drawn from the works it reviews: (1) context/instruction layer -- an attacker who can inject content via direct user prompts, indirect external content (web pages, documents, emails), or manipulated tool-return values, exploiting the model's inability to separate control instructions from untrusted data; (2) tool/action layer -- an attacker who manipulates tool descriptions/metadata to bias tool selection (e.g., ToolHijacker), crafts malicious arguments once a tool is chosen, or steers multi-step execution chains; (3) state/persistence layer -- an attacker who poisons persistent memory, contaminates accumulated interaction history, or exploits memory reuse/cross-session persistence so influence outlives the triggering session; (4) ecosystem/automation layer -- an attacker who compromises third-party skills/plugins (supply chain), exploits weak trust boundaries between composed components, abuses scheduled/event-driven automation (heartbeat, cron), or manipulates multi-agent communication. The OpenClaw case study (Section 5.4) is explicitly labeled by the authors as a 'plausible' scenario -- constructed from OpenClaw's public documentation, not an executed exploit or empirical finding -- illustrating how a workspace file (e.g., CLAUDE.md/bootstrap artifact) poisoned with adversarial instructions could, in principle, propagate through context assembly, typed-tool invocation, persistent workspace state, and heartbeat/cron automation into an ecosystem-level compromise.
limitations: As stated or clearly implied by the paper itself: (1) the survey performs no independent empirical validation -- all attack success rates, defense efficacy claims, and severity figures (e.g., the 6-7x/9x token-amplification figures attributed to Clawdrain, or ToolHijacker's tool-selection manipulation) are taken from cited third-party papers, not reproduced or verified by the authors; (2) the central OpenClaw case study (Section 5.4) is explicitly described by the authors as a 'plausible cross-layer attack scenario' constructed from OpenClaw's public documentation -- it is not a demonstrated exploit, proof-of-concept, or reported real-world incident; (3) the layer classification and study counts in Table 6 rest on the authors' own categorization of a hand-picked set of 19 prior studies, which is a subjective and non-exhaustive sample rather than a systematic/PRISMA-style literature review; (4) the paper itself flags, as open problems, that the literature it surveys (including implicitly its own analysis) has a research imbalance across layers, lacks long-horizon/multi-session evaluation methodology, and lacks standardized, enforceable ecosystem trust models -- these are gaps in the surveyed field that the paper does not close, only names.

quantitative_results: Table 6 (paper's own tabulation of 19 representative prior studies): context and tool layers together account for 13 of 19 studies; state layer accounts for 2 studies; ecosystem layer accounts for 2 studies.

Of the 19 studies in Table 6, only 2 (TrustRAG, TrustAgent) are classified by the authors as proposing a full defense (Y); 2 more (AgentDojo, ASB) are classified as offering a partial defense (P); the remainder are classified as proposing no defense (N).

Cited (not authors' own) finding attributed to Clawdrain [16]: tool-use workflow manipulation can amplify token consumption 6-7x, and up to 9x in certain configurations -- this number originates in the cited Clawdrain paper, not in original experiments run by this survey's authors.
